title: Wiring a 13 A plug
subtitle: Fit or replace a moulded 13 A plug on an appliance flex
excerpt: How to wire a standard three-pin 13 A BS 1363 plug on an appliance flex, covering stripping the flex outer sheath, preparing the conductor cores, connecting to the correct terminals, fitting the correct fuse rating, and checking the assembly.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics. BS 1363 (13 A plug standard). IET Wiring Regulations.

glossaryTerms.definition: The cartridge fuse inside a 13 A plug that protects the flex (not the appliance) from overcurrent. Standard ratings are 3 A (for appliances up to 700 W, including most lamp and low-power equipment flexes) and 13 A (for appliances over 700 W and up to the socket maximum). Fitting a 13 A fuse in a low-power appliance means the fuse will not blow when the flex is damaged; the correct fuse for a table lamp is 3 A.

The outer plastic or rubber layer of an appliance flex that contains all the conductor cores. The sheath must enter the cable clamp in the plug body and be gripped by it: only the individual conductor cores extend beyond the clamp. A flex where the outer sheath does not reach the clamp has no strain relief and the conductors will pull free from their terminals when the flex is tugged.

content.text: The three pins on a 13 A BS 1363 plug are labelled L (live, brown), N (neutral, blue), and E (earth, green and yellow). The earth pin is the longest, connects first, and disconnects last. The
is in the live pin, not the neutral. The
of the flex must be gripped by the cable clamp inside the plug body: this is the key safety detail that most DIY plug wiring gets wrong.

A replacement 13 A BS 1363 plug (the standard UK three-pin plug). The correct fuse: 3 A for appliances up to 700 W (lamps, phone chargers, radios), 13 A for appliances over 700 W (kettles, irons, toasters, power tools). Plugs sold in the UK may be supplied with a 13 A fuse pre-fitted; replace this with a 3 A fuse for low-power appliances.

Cut the damaged plug off the flex. Measure the distance from the cable clamp to the terminals in the new plug: this is how far the outer sheath must be stripped. Strip the outer sheath by scoring it with the utility knife along its length (take care not to cut into the cores inside) and peeling it back. The outer sheath must extend into the cable clamp with at least 5 mm to spare.

Strip 8 to 10 mm of insulation from the end of each conductor core with the wire strippers. Set the stripper gauge to the correct conductor size (1.0 mm² for most appliance flexes). Fan the strands of each core and twist them clockwise with the fingers to prevent stray strands bridging across terminals. Trim each core to length against the plug body so the core just reaches its terminal with no excess slack.

Connect each core to the correct terminal: brown to L (live, top left when the earth pin is at the bottom), blue to N (neutral, top right), and green-and-yellow to E (earth, bottom centre, the largest pin). On screw-down pillar terminals, fold the stripped conductor back on itself before inserting it into the terminal hole: this doubles the conductor contact area and prevents single strands from escaping. Tighten each screw firmly.
Step 4, clamp the flex and fit the correct fuse

Ensure the outer sheath is held firmly in the cable clamp. Tighten the cable clamp screw until the sheath cannot be pulled out: this is the strain relief and it protects the terminal connections. Fit the correct fuse in the fuse carrier and push the carrier back into the live pin slot. Replace the plug cover and tighten the central screw. Tug the flex gently to confirm the clamp is holding the sheath and not the individual cores.

A two-core flex (no earth core) is used on double-insulated appliances marked with the double-insulation symbol (a square within a square). The earth terminal in the plug is left empty. Only connect to the E terminal if the flex has a green-and-yellow earth core: do not add a wire to an earth terminal that the appliance does not require.

Check plugs and flexes on older appliances annually. A plug that runs warm to the touch after use has a loose terminal connection: the resistance at the joint generates heat. Open the plug, tighten all terminal screws, and check for any discolouration inside the plug body. Replace the plug if the inside is blackened or melted.

attrs.symptom: Fuse blows immediately when the plug is inserted
attrs.cause: The fuse rating is too low for the appliance load; or a strand of conductor is bridging between live and neutral terminals inside the plug.
attrs.fix: Remove the plug from the socket before opening it. Check for stray strands at the terminals. If the wiring is correct, check the wattage of the appliance and fit the appropriate fuse: 3 A for under 700 W, 13 A for over 700 W.

attrs.symptom: Flex pulls free from the plug when the lead is tugged
attrs.cause: The cable clamp was not tightened onto the outer sheath, or the sheath was stripped back too far and the clamp is gripping only the individual cores.
attrs.fix: Open the plug, cut the flex back to the correct length so the outer sheath extends into the clamp, re-strip, re-connect, and tighten the cable clamp screw firmly.
